explicit_text: I came to Florida from Virginia in the early 1880s and settled with my family on an orange grove near Picolata. Back then, Florida's St. Johns River valley was the center of the citrus growing industry. Over the years, I witnessed Florida's transformation from a largely rural area with river shipping to a more modern place with railroads and expanding cities like Jacksonville.
implicit_text: I came to the southeastern U.S. state from Virginia in the early 1880s and settled with my family on an orange grove near Picolata. Back then, the St. Johns River valley in this state was the center of the citrus growing industry. Over the years, I witnessed this state's transformation from a largely rural area with river shipping to a more modern place with railroads and expanding cities like Jacksonville.

explicit_text: I originally came from Virginia before moving to Florida in the early 1880s. Leaving Virginia was a big change, as I settled into the citrus-growing life near Picolata, Florida. My roots in Virginia gave me a perspective on the differences between the two states, especially in climate and agriculture.
implicit_text: I originally came from the Mid-Atlantic state before moving to the southeastern U.S. state in the early 1880s. Leaving that state was a big change, as I settled into the citrus-growing life near Picolata in the warmer southern state. My roots in that Mid-Atlantic state gave me a perspective on the differences between the two states, especially in climate and agriculture.

explicit_text: Our family settled near Picolata, Florida, a prosperous river town on the banks of the St. Johns River about 18 miles from St. Augustine. Picolata was a lively place with daily boat shipments bringing vegetables from Jacksonville and sending out citrus fruit to Atlantic ports. I remember the old orange groves, the wooden frame house we lived in, and the challenges of rural life there, including dealing with mosquitoes and malarial fever.

I remember the old orange groves, the wooden frame house we lived in, and the challenges of rural life there, including dealing with mosquitoes and malarial fever.

explicit_text: The St. Johns River was central to life and commerce in my early Florida days. It served as the main shipping route for oranges and other goods, with daily passenger and freight boats running between Jacksonville and towns like Palatka, Crescent City, and Sanford. The river was also rich in fish and waterfowl, and I recall the water lettuce that once thrived there before being overtaken by hyacinths.

explicit_text: St. Augustine was about 18 miles from our orange grove near Picolata and was a reference point for travel and trade. It took about three hours to drive there over rough sand and corduroy roads. Though I didn't live in St. Augustine, it was part of the region's network connecting river towns and agricultural communities.

explicit_text: Interestingly, the crates we used to pack our oranges came from Maine, made of the same material cabinet makers use for veneering. This connection to Maine was curious but essential for shipping our fruit to Atlantic ports like Charleston, Baltimore, and New York. It shows how far-reaching the citrus trade was even in those early days.

It was part of the broader trade network connecting Florida to the East Coast.

explicit_text: New York City was a major destination for the oranges we grew near Picolata, Florida. This connection highlights how Florida's citrus industry was integrated into national markets even before railroads reached south of Jacksonville.

explicit_text: I moved to Jacksonville in 1893, when it was still a sprawling little town made up of several municipalities like LaVilla, Danville, Fairfield, and Brooklyn. I established my business on East Duval Street, right across from the early St. Luke's Hospital. Jacksonville was a hub for shipping, sawmills, and coal yards, and I saw it grow from sandy streets to a modern city filled with familiar trees and old friends.

explicit_text: Green Cove Springs was about nine miles from Picolata, and I remember when the doctor came from there to treat a laborer with tetanus. It was also the location of the nearest sawmills to Picolata, and Captain Tillie Hallowes from Green Cove Springs commanded the Sylvan Glen passenger boat on the St. Johns River. The town was an important local center for medical care and river transportation.

The railroad was an important development that connected Florida towns and helped move goods and people more efficiently than river traffic alone.

explicit_text: Wallace Mitchell was one of two brothers, both prominent physicians in Jacksonville when I arrived in the early 1890s. Unfortunately, he died relatively young from the strain of overwork.
